003|## 1. Language & Project Info

006|- **Project Name:** python_chatbot_azure_vm

009|A Python backend program will be deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM, serving as the backend for a web-based chatbot. The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate responses. The chatbot frontend will be hosted on an Azure free web app, featuring a clean UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript. The frontend will communicate with the backend via HTTP API. The deployment environment is Azure, with the backend on a VM and the frontend on a free web app.
010|
011|## 2. Product Definition
012|### Product Goals
013|1. Provide a reliable, scalable Python backend for chatbot interactions, deployed cost-effectively on Azure VM.
014|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for high-quality conversational AI responses.
015|3. Deliver a clean, user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with robust communication between frontend and backend.
016|

018|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.
019|- As an admin, I want to deploy the backend on a low-cost Azure VM so that operational expenses are minimized.

Command Editor.insert_content_at_line executed: [File: /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/prd_python_chatbot_azure_vm.md (67 lines total after edit)]

001|# Product Requirement Document: Python Chatbot Backend on Azure VM
002|
003|## 1. Language & Project Info
004|- **Language:** English
005|- **Programming Language:** Python (Backend), HTML & JavaScript (Frontend)
006|- **Project Name:** python_chatbot_azure_vm
007|
008|### Restated Requirements
009|A Python backend program will be deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM, serving as the backend for a web-based chatbot. The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate responses. The chatbot frontend will be hosted on an Azure free web app, featuring a clean UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript. The frontend will communicate with the backend via HTTP API. The deployment environment is Azure, with the backend on a VM and the frontend on a free web app.
010|
011|## 2. Product Definition
012|### Product Goals
013|1. Provide a reliable, scalable Python backend for chatbot interactions, deployed cost-effectively on Azure VM.
014|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for high-quality conversational AI responses.
015|3. Deliver a clean, user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with robust communication between frontend and backend.
016|
017|### User Stories
018|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.
019|- As an admin, I want to deploy the backend on a low-cost Azure VM so that operational expenses are minimized.
020|- As a developer, I want the frontend to communicate with the backend via HTTP API so that integration is straightforward and maintainable.
021|- As a product owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that responses are intelligent and context-aware.
022|- As a user, I want the web app to have a clean and intuitive UI/UX so that my experience is enjoyable and efficient.
023|### Competitive Analysis
024|1. **Microsoft Bot Framework**
025|   - Pros: Deep Azure integration, scalable, supports multiple channels.
026|   - Cons: Complex setup, may require paid Azure services, less flexible for custom Python backends.
027|2. **Dialogflow (Google Cloud)**
028|   - Pros: Powerful NLP, easy integration, rich UI tools.
029|   - Cons: Not native to Azure, pricing can escalate, less control over backend.
030|3. **Rasa**
031|   - Pros: Open-source, customizable, supports Python, on-premise or cloud deployment.
032|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.
033|4. **Botpress**
034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.
035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.
036|5. **Azure Bot Service**
037|   - Pros: Native Azure support, easy deployment, scalable.
038|   - Cons: Limited free tier, less control over backend stack, may incur costs.
039|6. **Custom Flask/FastAPI Python Backend**
040|   - Pros: Full control, cost-effective on cheap VM, easy OpenAI API integration.
041|   - Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.
042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**
043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.
044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.
045|
046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart
047|```mermaid
048|quadrantChart
049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"
050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"
051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"
052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"
053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"
054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"
055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"
056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]
057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]
058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]
059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]
060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]
061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]
062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]
063|```
064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)
065|
066|## 3. Technical Specifications
067|(Sections to be completed: Requirements Analysis, Requirements Pool, UI Design Draft, Open Questions)

001|# Product Requirement Document: Python Chatbot Backend on Azure VM
002|
003|## 1. Language & Project Info
004|- **Language:** English
005|- **Programming Language:** Python (Backend), HTML & JavaScript (Frontend)
006|- **Project Name:** python_chatbot_azure_vm
007|
008|### Restated Requirements
009|A Python backend program will be deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM, serving as the backend for a web-based chatbot. The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate responses. The chatbot frontend will be hosted on an Azure free web app, featuring a clean UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript. The frontend will communicate with the backend via HTTP API. The deployment environment is Azure, with the backend on a VM and the frontend on a free web app.
010|
011|## 2. Product Definition
012|### Product Goals
013|1. Provide a reliable, scalable Python backend for chatbot interactions, deployed cost-effectively on Azure VM.
014|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for high-quality conversational AI responses.
015|3. Deliver a clean, user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with robust communication between frontend and backend.
016|
017|### User Stories
018|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.
019|- As an admin, I want to deploy the backend on a low-cost Azure VM so that operational expenses are minimized.
020|- As a developer, I want the frontend to communicate with the backend via HTTP API so that integration is straightforward and maintainable.
021|- As a product owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that responses are intelligent and context-aware.
022|- As a user, I want the web app to have a clean and intuitive UI/UX so that my experience is enjoyable and efficient.
023|### Competitive Analysis
024|1. **Microsoft Bot Framework**
025|   - Pros: Deep Azure integration, scalable, supports multiple channels.
026|   - Cons: Complex setup, may require paid Azure services, less flexible for custom Python backends.
027|2. **Dialogflow (Google Cloud)**
028|   - Pros: Powerful NLP, easy integration, rich UI tools.
029|   - Cons: Not native to Azure, pricing can escalate, less control over backend.
030|3. **Rasa**
031|   - Pros: Open-source, customizable, supports Python, on-premise or cloud deployment.
032|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.
033|4. **Botpress**
034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.
035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.
036|5. **Azure Bot Service**
037|   - Pros: Native Azure support, easy deployment, scalable.
038|   - Cons: Limited free tier, less control over backend stack, may incur costs.
039|6. **Custom Flask/FastAPI Python Backend**
040|   - Pros: Full control, cost-effective on cheap VM, easy OpenAI API integration.
041|   - Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.
042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**
043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.
044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.
045|
046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart
047|```mermaid
048|quadrantChart
049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"
050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"
051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"
052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"
053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"
054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"
055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"
056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]
057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]
058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]
059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]
060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]
061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]
062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]
063|```
064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)
065|
066|## 3. Technical Specifications
067|### Requirements Analysis
068|- The backend must be implemented in Python and deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM (e.g., B1s/B1ls tier).
069|- The backend must expose an HTTP API endpoint for chatbot interactions.
070|- The backend must securely interface with the OpenAI API for generating responses.
071|- The frontend must be hosted on Azure free web app, built with HTML and JavaScript, and communicate with the backend via HTTP API.
072|- The UI/UX must be clean, responsive, and intuitive, supporting real-time chat interactions.
073|- Security, scalability, and cost-efficiency are critical for both backend and frontend.
074|
075|### Requirements Pool
076|- **P0 (Must-have):**
077|  - Python backend on Azure VM
078|  - HTTP API for chatbot communication
079|  - OpenAI API integration
080|  - HTML/JavaScript frontend on Azure free web app
081|  - Clean, responsive UI/UX
082|- **P1 (Should-have):**
083|  - Basic authentication for backend API
084|  - Error handling and logging
085|  - Mobile-friendly design
086|- **P2 (Nice-to-have):**
087|  - Conversation history
088|  - Customizable chatbot avatar
089|  - Analytics dashboard
090|
091|### UI Design Draft
092|- **Layout:**
093|  - Header with chatbot title
094|  - Main chat window with message bubbles
095|  - Input box for user queries
096|  - Send button
097|- **Functionality:**
098|  - Real-time message display
099|  - Loading indicator for responses
100|  - Responsive design for desktop and mobile
101|
102|### Open Questions
103|- What is the expected user load (concurrent users)?
104|- Are there specific security or compliance requirements?
105|- Should the chatbot support multiple languages?
106|- Is persistent conversation history required?
107|- What branding elements (logo, colors) should be included in the UI?
108|(Sections to be completed: Requirements Analysis, Requirements Pool, UI Design Draft, Open Questions)
(this is the end of the file)

Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.\n042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**\n043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.\n044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.\n045|\n046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart\n047|```mermaid\n048|quadrantChart\n049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"\n050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"\n051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"\n052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"\n053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"\n054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"\n055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"\n056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]\n057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]\n058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]\n059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]\n060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]\n061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]\n062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]\n063|```\n064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)\n065|\n066|## 3. The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate responses. The chatbot frontend will be hosted on an Azure free web app, featuring a clean UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript. The frontend will communicate with the backend via HTTP API. The deployment environment is Azure, with the backend on a VM and the frontend on a free web app.\n010|\n011|## 2. Product Definition\n012|### Product Goals\n013|1. Provide a reliable, scalable Python backend for chatbot interactions, deployed cost-effectively on Azure VM.\n014|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for high-quality conversational AI responses.\n015|3. Deliver a clean, user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with robust communication between frontend and backend.\n016|\n017|### User Stories\n018|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.\n019|- As an admin, I want to deploy the backend on a low-cost Azure VM so that operational expenses are minimized.\n020|- As a developer, I want the frontend to communicate with the backend via HTTP API so that integration is straightforward and maintainable.\n021|- As a product owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that responses are intelligent and context-aware.\n022|- As a user, I want the web app to have a clean and intuitive UI/UX so that my experience is enjoyable and efficient.\n023|### Competitive Analysis\n024|1. **Microsoft Bot Framework**\n025|   - Pros: Deep Azure integration, scalable, supports multiple channels.\n026|   - Cons: Complex setup, may require paid Azure services, less flexible for custom Python backends.\n027|2. **Dialogflow (Google Cloud)**\n028|   - Pros: Powerful NLP, easy integration, rich UI tools.\n029|   - Cons: Not native to Azure, pricing can escalate, less control over backend.\n030|3. **Rasa**\n031|   - Pros: Open-source, customizable, supports Python, on-premise or cloud deployment.\n032|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.\n033|4. **Botpress**\n034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.\n035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.\n036|5. **Azure Bot Service**\n037|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.\\n033|4. **Botpress**\\n034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.\\n035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.\\n036|5. **Azure Bot Service**\\n037|   - Pros: Native Azure support, easy deployment, scalable.\\n038|   - Cons: Limited free tier, less control over backend stack, may incur costs.\\n039|6. **Custom Flask/FastAPI Python Backend**\\n040|   - Pros: Full control, cost-effective on cheap VM, easy OpenAI API integration.\\n041|   - Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.\\n042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**\\n043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.\\n044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.\\n045|\\n046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart\\n047|```mermaid\\n048|quadrantChart\\n049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"\\n050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"\\n051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"\\n052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"\\n053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"\\n054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"\\n055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"\\n056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]\\n057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]\\n058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]\\n059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]\\n060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]\\n061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]\\n062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]\\n063|```\\n064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)\\n065|\\n066|## 3. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for high-quality conversational AI responses.\\n015|3. Deliver a clean, user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with robust communication between frontend and backend.\\n016|\\n017|### User Stories\\n018|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.\\n019|- As an admin, I want to deploy the backend on a low-cost Azure VM so that operational expenses are minimized.\\n020|- As a developer, I want the frontend to communicate with the backend via HTTP API so that integration is straightforward and maintainable.\\n021|- As a product owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that responses are intelligent and context-aware.\\n022|- As a user, I want the web app to have a clean and intuitive UI/UX so that my experience is enjoyable and efficient.\\n023|### Competitive Analysis\\n024|1. **Microsoft Bot Framework**\\n025|   - Pros: Deep Azure integration, scalable, supports multiple channels.\\n026|   - Cons: Complex setup, may require paid Azure services, less flexible for custom Python backends.\\n027|2. **Dialogflow (Google Cloud)**\\n028|   - Pros: Powerful NLP, easy integration, rich UI tools.\\n029|   - Cons: Not native to Azure, pricing can escalate, less control over backend.\\n030|3. **Rasa**\\n031|   - Pros: Open-source, customizable, supports Python, on-premise or cloud deployment.\\n032|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.\\n033|4. **Botpress**\\n034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.\\n035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.\\n036|5. **Azure Bot Service**\\n037|   - Pros: Native Azure support, easy deployment, scalable.\\n038|   - Cons: Limited free tier, less control over backend stack, may incur costs.\\n039|6. **Custom Flask/FastAPI Python Backend**\\n040|   - Pros: Full control, cost-effective on cheap VM, easy OpenAI API integration.\\n041|   - Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.\\n042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**\\n043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.\\n044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.\\n045|\\n046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart\\n047|```mermaid\\n048|quadrantChart\\n049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"\\n050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"\\n051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"\\n052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"\\n053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"\\n054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"\\n055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"\\n056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]\\n057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]\\n058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]\\n059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]\\n060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]\\n061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]\\n062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]\\n063|```\\n064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)\\n065|\\n066|## 3. 2025-9-20 15:03:14.036 | INFO     | metagpt.roles.di.role_zero:_act:294 - Commands outputs: 
Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/python_chatbot_azure_vm/backend/main.py has been successfully created, with content:
import os
import datetime
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, Depends
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from pydantic import BaseModel
from starlette.status import HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED
from utils import OpenAIClient
from auth import AuthManager
from logging_config import setup_logger, log_request, log_response

# Load environment variables
load_dotenv()
OPENAI_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
API_AUTH_TOKEN = os.getenv("API_AUTH_TOKEN")  # For basic token authentication

if not OPENAI_API_KEY:
    raise RuntimeError("OPENAI_API_KEY not set in .env file")
if not API_AUTH_TOKEN:
    raise RuntimeError("API_AUTH_TOKEN not set in .env file")

# Initialize FastAPI app
app = FastAPI()

# Setup CORS (allow frontend origin, adjust as needed)
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],  # For production, specify allowed origins
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

# Setup logger
logger = setup_logger()

# Data models
class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    user_id: str
    message: str

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    response: str
    timestamp: datetime.datetime

# Dependency for authentication
def get_token_header(request: Request):
    auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")
    if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Missing or invalid Authorization header",
        )
    token = auth_header.split("Bearer ")[1]
    auth_manager = AuthManager(API_AUTH_TOKEN)
    if not auth_manager.verify_token(token):
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Invalid authentication token",
        )
    return token

# Health check endpoint
@app.get("/health_check")
async def health_check():
    return JSONResponse(content={"status": "ok"})

# Chat endpoint
@app.post("/chat", response_model=ChatResponse)
async def chat_endpoint(request: ChatRequest, token: str = Depends(get_token_header)):
    log_request(logger, request)
    openai_client = OpenAIClient(OPENAI_API_KEY)
    try:
        response_text = await openai_client.generate_response(request.message)
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"OpenAI API error: {str(e)}")
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Error communicating with OpenAI API")
    chat_response = ChatResponse(
        response=response_text,
        timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow()
    )
    log_response(logger, chat_response)
    return chat_response

# Root endpoint (optional)
@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return JSONResponse(content={"message": "Chatbot backend is running."})

an existing plan to achieve the goal. A plan consists of one to 3 tasks.\nIf plan is created, you should track the progress and update the plan accordingly, such as Plan.finish_current_task, Plan.append_task, Plan.reset_task, Plan.replace_task, etc.\nWhen presented a current task, tackle the task using the available commands.\nPay close attention to new user message, review the conversation history, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to respond to new user requirement.\nNote:\n1. setup_logger, log_request, log_response\n\n# Load environment variables\nload_dotenv()\nOPENAI_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")\nAPI_AUTH_TOKEN = os.getenv("API_AUTH_TOKEN")  # For basic token authentication\n\nif not OPENAI_API_KEY:\n    raise RuntimeError("OPENAI_API_KEY not set in .env file")\nif not API_AUTH_TOKEN:\n    raise RuntimeError("API_AUTH_TOKEN not set in .env file")\n\n# Initialize FastAPI app\napp = FastAPI()\n\n# Setup CORS (allow frontend origin, adjust as needed)\napp.add_middleware(\n    CORSMiddleware,\n    allow_origins=["*"],  # For production, specify allowed origins\n    allow_credentials=True,\n    allow_methods=["*"],\n    allow_headers=["*"],\n)\n\n# Setup logger\nlogger = setup_logger()\n\n# Data models\nclass ChatRequest(BaseModel):\n    user_id: str\n    message: str\n\nclass ChatResponse(BaseModel):\n    response: str\n    timestamp: datetime.datetime\n\n# Dependency for authentication\ndef get_token_header(request: Request):\n    auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")\n    if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):\n        raise HTTPException(\n            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,\n            detail="Missing or invalid Authorization header",\n        )\n    token = auth_header.split("Bearer ")[1]\n    auth_manager = AuthManager(API_AUTH_TOKEN)\n    if not auth_manager.verify_token(token):\n        raise HTTPException(\n            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,\n            detail="Invalid authentication token",\n        )\n    return token\n\n# Health check endpoint\n@app.get("/health_check")\nasync def health_check():\n    return JSONResponse(content={"status": "ok"})\n\n# Chat endpoint\n@app.post("/chat", response_model=ChatResponse)\nasync def chat_endpoint(request: ChatRequest, token: str = Depends(get_token_header)):\n    log_request(logger, request)\n    openai_client = OpenAIClient(OPENAI_API_KEY)\n    try:\n        response_text = await openai_client.generate_response(request.message)\n    except Exception as e:\n        logger.error(f"OpenAI API error: {str(e)}")\n        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="Error communicating with OpenAI API")\n    chat_response = ChatResponse(\n        response=response_text,\n        timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow()\n    )\n    log_response(logger, chat_response)\n    return chat_response\n\n# Root endpoint (optional)\n@app.get("/")\nasync def root():\n    return JSONResponse(content={"message": "Chatbot backend is running."})\n\nCommand Plan.finish_current_task executed:Current task is finished. Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/python_chatbot_azure_vm/backend/utils.py has been successfully created, with content:
import os
import aiohttp

class OpenAIClient:
    """
    Handles communication with the OpenAI API.
    """

    def __init__(self, api_key: str):
        if not api_key:
            raise ValueError("OpenAI API key must be provided.")
        self.api_key = api_key
        self.api_url = "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions"
        self.model = "gpt-3.5-turbo"  # You may change this to another model if needed

    async def generate_response(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        """
        Sends a prompt to the OpenAI API and returns the response text.
        """
        headers = {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}",
            "Content-Type": "application/json"
        }
        payload = {
            "model": self.model,
            "messages": [
                {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
            ],
            "max_tokens": 256,
            "temperature": 0.7
        }

        async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
            async with session.post(self.api_url, json=payload, headers=headers) as resp:
                if resp.status != 200:
                    error_detail = await resp.text()
                    raise Exception(f"OpenAI API error: {resp.status} {error_detail}")
                data = await resp.json()
                # Extract the response from the API
                try:
                    return data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()
                except (KeyError, IndexError):
                    raise Exception("Malformed response from OpenAI API.")

Pros: Deep Azure integration, scalable, supports multiple channels.\\n026|   - Cons: Complex setup, may require paid Azure services, less flexible for custom Python backends.\\n027|2. **Dialogflow (Google Cloud)**\\n028|   - Cons: Requires manual setup, not optimized for Azure, UI/UX not included.\\n033|4. **Botpress**\\n034|   - Pros: Open-source, modular, supports custom backend logic.\\n035|   - Cons: Node.js based, not Python, Azure deployment requires extra configuration.\\n036|5. **Azure Bot Service**\\n037|   - Pros: Native Azure support, easy deployment, scalable.\\n038|   - Cons: Limited free tier, less control over backend stack, may incur costs.\\n039|6. **Custom Flask/FastAPI Python Backend**\\n040|   - Pros: Full control, cost-effective on cheap VM, easy OpenAI API integration.\\n041|   - Cons: Requires manual deployment, security and scaling handled by developer.\\n042|7. **Streamlit Chatbot**\\n043|   - Pros: Rapid prototyping, Python-based, simple UI.\\n044|   - Cons: Not production-grade, limited UI/UX customization, not optimized for Azure web app.\\n045|\\n046|### Competitive Quadrant Chart\\n047|```mermaid\\n048|quadrantChart\\n049|    title "Chatbot Backend Solutions: Flexibility vs. Cost"\\n050|    x-axis "Low Flexibility" --> "High Flexibility"\\n051|    y-axis "High Cost" --> "Low Cost"\\n052|    quadrant-1 "Best for Customization"\\n053|    quadrant-2 "Best for Enterprise"\\n054|    quadrant-3 "Best for Rapid Prototyping"\\n055|    quadrant-4 "Best for Budget"\\n056|    "Microsoft Bot Framework": [0.3, 0.3]\\n057|    "Dialogflow": [0.4, 0.4]\\n058|    "Rasa": [0.8, 0.5]\\n059|    "Botpress": [0.7, 0.6]\\n060|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.5, 0.2]\\n061|    "Custom Python Backend (Target)": [0.9, 0.8]\\n062|    "Streamlit Chatbot": [0.6, 0.7]\\n063|```\\n064|(Sections to be completed: Product Goals, User Stories, Competitive Analysis, Competitive Quadrant Chart)\\n065|\\n066|## 3. You may change this to another model if needed\n\n    async def generate_response(self, prompt: str) -> str:\n        """\n        Sends a prompt to the OpenAI API and returns the response text.\n        """\n        headers = {\n            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}",\n            "Content-Type": "application/json"\n        }\n        payload = {\n            "model": self.model,\n            "messages": [\n                {"role": "user", "content": prompt}\n            ],\n            "max_tokens": 256,\n            "temperature": 0.7\n        }\n\n        async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:\n            async with session.post(self.api_url, json=payload, headers=headers) as resp:\n                if resp.status != 200:\n                    error_detail = await resp.text()\n                    raise Exception(f"OpenAI API error: {resp.status} {error_detail}")\n                data = await resp.json()\n                # auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")\n    if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):\n        raise HTTPException(\n            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,\n            detail="Missing or invalid Authorization header",\n        )\n    token = auth_header.split("Bearer ")[1]\n    auth_manager = AuthManager(API_AUTH_TOKEN)\n    if not auth_manager.verify_token(token):\n        raise HTTPException(\n            status_code=HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,\n            detail="Invalid authentication token",\n        )\n    return token\n\n# You may change this to another model if needed\n\n    async def generate_response(self, prompt: str) -> str:\n        """\n        Sends a prompt to the OpenAI API and returns the response text.\n        """\n        headers = {\n            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}",\n            "Content-Type": "application/json"\n        }\n        payload = {\n            "model": self.model,\n            "messages": [\n                {"role": "user", "content": prompt}\n            ],\n            "max_tokens": 256,\n            "temperature": 0.7\n        }\n\n        async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:\n            async with session.post(self.api_url, json=payload, headers=headers) as resp:\n                if resp.status != 200:\n                    error_detail = await resp.text()\n                    raise Exception(f"OpenAI API error: {resp.status} {error_detail}")\n                data = await resp.json()\n                # Extract the response from the API\n                try:\n                    return data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()\n                except (KeyError, IndexError):\n                    raise Exception("Malformed response from OpenAI API.")'}, {'role': 'user', 'content': "\n# User Requirement\n[Message] from Mike to Alex: Please implement the backend in Python as per the system design, deploy it on a cheap Azure VM, and ensure it exposes the required HTTP endpoints for the chatbot. class AuthManager:
    """
    Handles basic token authentication for the backend API.
    """

    def __init__(self, valid_token: str):
        if not valid_token:
            raise ValueError("A valid API authentication token must be provided.")
        self.valid_token = valid_token

    def verify_token(self, token: str) -> bool:
        """
        Verifies that the provided token matches the expected API token.
        """
        return token == self.valid_token

Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/python_chatbot_azure_vm/backend/logging_config.py has been successfully created, with content:
import logging
from typing import Any
from datetime import datetime
from .utils import OpenAIClient  # For type hints if needed

def setup_logger(log_file: str = "chatbot_backend.log") -> logging.Logger:
    """
    Sets up and returns a logger for the backend.
    Logs to both console and a file.
    """
    logger = logging.getLogger("chatbot_backend")
    logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    formatter = logging.Formatter(
        "[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s in %(module)s: %(message)s"
    )

    # File handler
    file_handler = logging.FileHandler(log_file)
    file_handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    logger.addHandler(file_handler)

    # Console handler
    console_handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    console_handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    logger.addHandler(console_handler)

    # Avoid duplicate logs
    logger.propagate = False

    return logger

def log_request(logger: logging.Logger, request: Any) -> None:
    """
    Logs the incoming chat request.
    """
    logger.info(f"Received request: user_id={getattr(request, 'user_id', None)}, message={getattr(request, 'message', None)}")

def log_response(logger: logging.Logger, response: Any) -> None:
    """
    Logs the outgoing chat response.
    """
    logger.info(f"Sent response: response={getattr(response, 'response', None)}, timestamp={getattr(response, 'timestamp', None)}")

an existing plan to achieve the goal. A plan consists of one to 3 tasks.\nIf plan is created, you should track the progress and update the plan accordingly, such as Plan.finish_current_task, Plan.append_task, Plan.reset_task, Plan.replace_task, etc.\nWhen presented a current task, tackle the task using the available commands.\nPay close attention to new user message, review the conversation history, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to respond to new user requirement.\nNote:\n1. If you keeping encountering errors, unexpected situation, or you are not sure of proceeding, use RoleZero.ask_human to ask for help.\n2. Carefully review your progress at the current task, if your actions so far has not fulfilled the task instruction, you should continue with current task. 001|# Azure Chatbot PRD
002|
003|## 1. Language & Project Info
004|- **Language:** English
005|- **Programming Language:** Python (backend), HTML/JavaScript (frontend)
006|- **Project Name:** azure_chatbot_backend
007|- **Restated Requirements:**
008|  - Implement a Python program deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM to serve as the backend for a web-based chatbot.
009|  - The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate chatbot responses.
010|  - The chatbot should be accessible via an Azure free web app, featuring a clean and intuitive UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript.
011|
012|## 2. Product Definition
013|### Product Goals
014|1. Deliver a reliable Python backend chatbot service hosted on a low-cost Azure VM.
015|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for intelligent conversational capabilities.
016|3. Provide a user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with clean UI/UX using HTML and JavaScript.
017|
018|### User Stories
019|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.
020|- As a developer, I want the backend to be easily deployable on a cheap Azure VM so that operational costs remain low.
021|- As a business owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that conversations are intelligent and context-aware.
022|- As a user, I want the web app to be accessible from any device so that I can use the chatbot conveniently.
023|- As a designer, I want the UI/UX to be clean and intuitive so that users have a pleasant experience.
024|### Competitive Analysis
025|
026|| Product                | Pros                                              | Cons                                              |
027||------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
028|| Azure Bot Service      | Native Azure integration, scalable, easy setup    | Can be costly at scale, limited free tier         |
029|| Dialogflow (Google)    | Powerful NLP, multi-language support              | Complex setup, Google Cloud dependency            |
030|| IBM Watson Assistant   | Advanced AI, enterprise features                  | Expensive, steep learning curve                   |
031|| Botpress               | Open-source, customizable                        | Requires self-hosting, less cloud integration     |
032|| Rasa                   | Open-source, strong customization                 | No managed hosting, setup complexity              |
033|| ManyChat               | Easy to use, good for marketing                   | Limited AI, not suitable for custom backend       |
034|| Tidio                  | Simple UI, integrates with websites               | Limited AI, not Python-based                      |
035|
036|#### Competitive Quadrant Chart
037|```mermaid
038|quadrantChart
039|    title "Chatbot Platform Comparison"
040|    x-axis "Low Cost" --> "High Cost"
041|    y-axis "Low Customization" --> "High Customization"
042|    quadrant-1 "Best for Custom, Low Cost"
043|    quadrant-2 "Custom, High Cost"
044|    quadrant-3 "Low Cost, Limited Custom"
045|    quadrant-4 "High Cost, Limited Custom"
046|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.7, 0.6]
047|    "Dialogflow": [0.8, 0.7]
048|    "IBM Watson Assistant": [0.9, 0.8]
049|    "Botpress": [0.3, 0.9]
050|    "Rasa": [0.2, 1.0]
051|    "ManyChat": [0.4, 0.3]
052|    "Tidio": [0.5, 0.4]
053|    "Our Target Product": [0.1, 0.8]
054|```

Example:\n```mermaid\nquadrantChart\n    title "Reach and engagement of campaigns"\n    x-axis "Low Reach" --> "High Reach"\n    y-axis "Low Engagement" --> "High Engagement"\n    quadrant-1 "We should expand"\n    quadrant-2 "Need to promote"\n    quadrant-3 "Re-evaluate"\n    quadrant-4 "May be improved"\n    "Campaign A": [0.3, 0.6]\n    "Campaign B": [0.45, 0.23]\n    "Campaign C": [0.57, 0.69]\n    "Campaign D": [0.78, 0.34]\n    "Campaign E": [0.40, 0.34]\n    "Campaign F": [0.35, 0.78]\n    "Our Target Product": [0.5, 0.6]\n```\n\n### PRD Document Guidelines\n- Use clear requirement language (Must/Should/May)\n- Include measurable criteria\n- Prioritize clearly (P0: Must-have, P1: Should-have, P2: Nice-to-have)\n- Support with diagrams and charts\n- Focus on user value and business goals\n\n## Mode 2: Market Research\nTriggered by market analysis or competitor research requests, ending with the output of a complete report document.\n\n### **IMPORTANT** Information Collection Requirements\n\nMust follow this strict information gathering process:\n1. 001|# Azure Chatbot PRD
002|
003|## 1. Language & Project Info
004|- **Language:** English
005|- **Programming Language:** Python (backend), HTML/JavaScript (frontend)
006|- **Project Name:** azure_chatbot_backend
007|- **Restated Requirements:**
008|  - Implement a Python program deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM to serve as the backend for a web-based chatbot.
009|  - The backend must interface with the OpenAI API to process and generate chatbot responses.
010|  - The chatbot should be accessible via an Azure free web app, featuring a clean and intuitive UI/UX designed using HTML and JavaScript.
011|
012|## 2. Product Definition
013|### Product Goals
014|1. Deliver a reliable Python backend chatbot service hosted on a low-cost Azure VM.
015|2. Ensure seamless integration with the OpenAI API for intelligent conversational capabilities.
016|3. Provide a user-friendly web interface accessible via Azure free web app, with clean UI/UX using HTML and JavaScript.
017|
018|### User Stories
019|- As a user, I want to interact with a chatbot through a web interface so that I can get instant responses to my queries.
020|- As a developer, I want the backend to be easily deployable on a cheap Azure VM so that operational costs remain low.
021|- As a business owner, I want the chatbot to leverage the OpenAI API so that conversations are intelligent and context-aware.
022|- As a user, I want the web app to be accessible from any device so that I can use the chatbot conveniently.
023|- As a designer, I want the UI/UX to be clean and intuitive so that users have a pleasant experience.
024|### Competitive Analysis
025|
026|| Product                | Pros                                              | Cons                                              |
027||------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
028|| Azure Bot Service      | Native Azure integration, scalable, easy setup    | Can be costly at scale, limited free tier         |
029|| Dialogflow (Google)    | Powerful NLP, multi-language support              | Complex setup, Google Cloud dependency            |
030|| IBM Watson Assistant   | Advanced AI, enterprise features                  | Expensive, steep learning curve                   |
031|| Botpress               | Open-source, customizable                        | Requires self-hosting, less cloud integration     |
032|| Rasa                   | Open-source, strong customization                 | No managed hosting, setup complexity              |
033|| ManyChat               | Easy to use, good for marketing                   | Limited AI, not suitable for custom backend       |
034|| Tidio                  | Simple UI, integrates with websites               | Limited AI, not Python-based                      |
035|
036|#### Competitive Quadrant Chart
037|```mermaid
038|quadrantChart
039|    title "Chatbot Platform Comparison"
040|    x-axis "Low Cost" --> "High Cost"
041|    y-axis "Low Customization" --> "High Customization"
042|    quadrant-1 "Best for Custom, Low Cost"
043|    quadrant-2 "Custom, High Cost"
044|    quadrant-3 "Low Cost, Limited Custom"
045|    quadrant-4 "High Cost, Limited Custom"
046|    "Azure Bot Service": [0.7, 0.6]
047|    "Dialogflow": [0.8, 0.7]
048|    "IBM Watson Assistant": [0.9, 0.8]
049|    "Botpress": [0.3, 0.9]
050|    "Rasa": [0.2, 1.0]
051|    "ManyChat": [0.4, 0.3]
052|    "Tidio": [0.5, 0.4]
053|    "Our Target Product": [0.1, 0.8]
054|```
055|## 3. Technical Specifications
056|
057|### Requirements Analysis
058|- The backend must be implemented in Python and deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM (e.g., B1s or similar low-tier instance).
059|- The backend must expose RESTful endpoints for chatbot interaction.
060|- Integration with OpenAI API is required for generating chatbot responses.
061|- The frontend must be hosted on Azure free web app, built with HTML and JavaScript, and communicate with the backend via HTTP requests.
062|- The UI/UX must be clean, responsive, and intuitive, supporting desktop and mobile browsers.
063|- Security measures must be in place to protect API keys and user data.
064|- The solution must be scalable to handle moderate traffic.
065|
066|### Requirements Pool
067|- **P0 (Must-have):**
068|  - Python backend deployed on Azure VM
069|  - RESTful API for chatbot communication
070|  - OpenAI API integration
071|  - HTML/JavaScript frontend hosted on Azure free web app
072|  - Clean, responsive UI/UX
073|- **P1 (Should-have):**
074|  - Basic user authentication
075|  - Logging and monitoring
076|  - Error handling and graceful degradation
077|- **P2 (Nice-to-have):**
078|  - Conversation history for users
079|  - Customizable chatbot persona
080|  - Multi-language support
081|
082|### UI Design Draft
083|```
084|+-------------------------------+
085||        Azure Chatbot          |
086|+-------------------------------+
087|| [Chat Window]                 |
088||  -------------------------    |
089|| | User: Hello!           |    |
090|| | Bot: Hi, how can I...  |    |
091||  -------------------------    |
092|| [Input Box] [Send Button]     |
093|+-------------------------------+
094|| [Footer: Powered by Azure & OpenAI] |
095|+-------------------------------+
096|```
097|
098|### Open Questions
099|- What is the expected peak user load?
100|- Are there specific compliance or data privacy requirements?
101|- Should the chatbot support file uploads or multimedia?
102|- Is there a preferred authentication method?
103|- What branding elements should be included in the UI?

Example:\n```mermaid\nquadrantChart\n    title "Reach and engagement of campaigns"\n    x-axis "Low Reach" --> "High Reach"\n    y-axis "Low Engagement" --> "High Engagement"\n    quadrant-1 "We should expand"\n    quadrant-2 "Need to promote"\n    quadrant-3 "Re-evaluate"\n    quadrant-4 "May be improved"\n    "Campaign A": [0.3, 0.6]\n    "Campaign B": [0.45, 0.23]\n    "Campaign C": [0.57, 0.69]\n    "Campaign D": [0.78, 0.34]\n    "Campaign E": [0.40, 0.34]\n    "Campaign F": [0.35, 0.78]\n    "Our Target Product": [0.5, 0.6]\n```\n\n### PRD Document Guidelines\n- Use clear requirement language (Must/Should/May)\n- Include measurable criteria\n- Prioritize clearly (P0: Must-have, P1: Should-have, P2: Nice-to-have)\n- Support with diagrams and charts\n- Focus on user value and business goals\n\n## Mode 2: Market Research\nTriggered by market analysis or competitor research requests, ending with the output of a complete report document.\n\n### **IMPORTANT** Information Collection Requirements\n\nMust follow this strict information gathering process:\n1. 2025-9-20 18:35:28.467 | INFO     | metagpt.roles.di.role_zero:_act:294 - Commands outputs: 
Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/azure_chatbot_backend/backend/main.py has been successfully created, with content:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from openai_client import get_chatbot_response

# Load environment variables from .env file if present
load_dotenv()

# FastAPI app initialization
app = FastAPI(
    title="Azure Chatbot Backend",
    description="Backend for web chatbot interfacing with OpenAI API",
    version="1.0.0"
)

# CORS configuration
ALLOWED_ORIGINS = os.getenv("ALLOWED_ORIGINS", "*").split(",")
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=ALLOWED_ORIGINS,
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    message: str

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    response: str

@app.post("/chat", response_model=ChatResponse)
async def chat_endpoint(chat_request: ChatRequest):
    """
    Receives a user message, sends it to OpenAI API, and returns the chatbot response.
    """
    user_message = chat_request.message.strip()
    if not user_message:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Message cannot be empty.")

    try:
        bot_response = await get_chatbot_response(user_message)
        return ChatResponse(response=bot_response)
    except Exception as e:
        return JSONResponse(
            status_code=500,
            content={"detail": f"Error communicating with OpenAI API: {str(e)}"}
        )

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return {"status": "ok", "message": "Azure Chatbot Backend is running."}

Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/azure_chatbot_backend/backend/openai_client.py has been successfully created, with content:
import os
import httpx
import asyncio

OPENAI_API_KEY = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
OPENAI_API_URL = "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions"
OPENAI_MODEL = os.getenv("OPENAI_MODEL", "gpt-3.5-turbo")

if not OPENAI_API_KEY:
    raise RuntimeError("OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable not set.")

async def get_chatbot_response(message: str) -> str:
    """
    Sends the user message to OpenAI's chat completion endpoint and returns the chatbot's response.
    """
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {OPENAI_API_KEY}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }
    payload = {
        "model": OPENAI_MODEL,
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
            {"role": "user", "content": message}
        ],
        "temperature": 0.7,
        "max_tokens": 512,
        "n": 1,
        "stream": False
    }

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        response = await client.post(OPENAI_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload)
        response.raise_for_status()
        data = response.json()
        # Extract the chatbot's reply
        try:
            return data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"].strip()
        except (KeyError, IndexError):
            raise RuntimeError("Invalid response from OpenAI API.")

2025-9-20 18:35:44.419 | INFO     | metagpt.roles.di.role_zero:_act:294 - Commands outputs: 
Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/azure_chatbot_backend/backend/utils.py has been successfully created, with content:
import logging
from typing import Optional

# Configure logging for the backend
logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.INFO,
    format="%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(name)s: %(message)s"
)
logger = logging.getLogger("azure_chatbot_backend")

def validate_user_message(message: Optional[str]) -> str:
    """
    Validates the user message input.
    Returns the stripped message if valid, raises ValueError otherwise.
    """
    if message is None:
        raise ValueError("Message cannot be None.")
    message = message.strip()
    if not message:
        raise ValueError("Message cannot be empty.")
    return message

def log_error(error: Exception, context: Optional[str] = None) -> None:
    """
    Logs an error with optional context.
    """
    if context:
        logger.error(f"{context}: {str(error)}")
    else:
        logger.error(str(error))

def log_info(info: str, context: Optional[str] = None) -> None:
    """
    Logs an info message with optional context.
    """
    if context:
        logger.info(f"{context}: {info}")
    else:
        logger.info(info)

2025-9-20 18:35:49.637 | INFO     | metagpt.roles.di.role_zero:_act:294 - Commands outputs: 
Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/azure_chatbot_backend/backend/Dockerfile has been successfully created, with content:
# Use official Python base image
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Expose port for FastAPI
EXPOSE 8000

# Set environment variables for production
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Start the FastAPI app using Gunicorn with Uvicorn workers

# Azure Chatbot Backend

This is the backend for a web-based chatbot, implemented in Python using FastAPI. It interfaces securely with the OpenAI API to provide conversational AI responses. The backend is designed to be deployed on a cost-effective Azure VM and communicates with a static frontend web app (HTML/JavaScript) hosted on Azure App Service Static Web Apps.

## Features

- FastAPI RESTful backend for chatbot communication
- Asynchronous calls to OpenAI API (GPT-3.5-turbo by default)
- Secure API key management via environment variables
- CORS configuration for safe frontend-backend communication
- Containerized with Docker for easy deployment
- Logging and input validation utilities

an existing plan to achieve the goal. A plan consists of one to 3 tasks.\nIf plan is created, you should track the progress and update the plan accordingly, such as Plan.finish_current_task, Plan.append_task, Plan.reset_task, Plan.replace_task, etc.\nWhen presented a current task, tackle the task using the available commands.\nPay close attention to new user message, review the conversation history, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to respond to new user requirement.\nNote:\n1. If you keeping encountering errors, unexpected situation, or you are not sure of proceeding, use RoleZero.ask_human to ask for help.\n2. Carefully review your progress at the current task, if your actions so far has not fulfilled the task instruction, you should continue with current task. Otherwise, finish current task by Plan.finish_current_task explicitly.\n3. Each time you finish a task, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to report your progress.\n4. Don\'t forget to append task first when all existing tasks are finished and new tasks are required.\n5. Avoid repeating tasks you have already completed. And end loop when all requirements are met.\nYou are an autonomous programmer\n\nThe special interface consists of a file editor that shows you 100 lines of a file at a time.\n\nYou can use terminal commands (e.g., cat, ls, cd) by calling Terminal.run_command.\n\nYou should carefully observe the behavior and results of the previous action, and avoid triggering repeated errors.\n\nIn addition to the terminal, I also provide additional tools. \n\nIf provided an issue link, you first action must be navigate to the issue page using Browser tool to understand the issue.\n\nYour must check if the repository exists at the current path. If it exists, navigate to the repository path. If the repository doesn\'t exist, please download it and then navigate to it.\nAll subsequent actions must be performed within this repository path. Do not leave this directory to execute any actions at any time.\n\nNote:\n\n1. If you open a file and need to get to an area around a specific line that is not in the first 100 lines, say line 583, don\'t just use the scroll_down command multiple times. Instead, use the Editor.goto_line command. It\'s much quicker. \n2. Always make sure to look at the currently open file and the current working directory (which appears right after the currently open file). The currently open file might be in a different directory than the working directory! Note that some commands, such as \'create\', open files, so they might change the current open file.\n3. When using Editor.edit_file_by_replace, if there is no exact match, take the difference in indentation into consideration.\n4. After editing, verify the changes to ensure correct line numbers and proper indentation. Adhere to PEP8 standards for Python code.\n5. NOTE ABOUT THE EDIT COMMAND: Indentation really matters! an existing plan to achieve the goal. A plan consists of one to 3 tasks.\nIf plan is created, you should track the progress and update the plan accordingly, such as Plan.finish_current_task, Plan.append_task, Plan.reset_task, Plan.replace_task, etc.\nWhen presented a current task, tackle the task using the available commands.\nPay close attention to new user message, review the conversation history, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to respond to new user requirement.\nNote:\n1. If you keeping encountering errors, unexpected situation, or you are not sure of proceeding, use RoleZero.ask_human to ask for help.\n2. Carefully review your progress at the current task, if your actions so far has not fulfilled the task instruction, you should continue with current task. Otherwise, finish current task by Plan.finish_current_task explicitly.\n3. Each time you finish a task, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to report your progress.\n4. Command Engineer2.write_new_code executed: The file /home/metagpt-fuzzing/Desktop/MetaGPT/workspace/azure_chatbot_backend/frontend/app.js has been successfully created, with content:
/**
 * app.js - Chatbot frontend logic
 * Handles user input, communicates with backend, updates UI, and manages errors.
 */

const BACKEND_URL = "https://<YOUR_BACKEND_DOMAIN_OR_IP>:8000/chat"; // Replace with your backend URL

// DOM Elements
const chatWindow = document.getElementById("chat-window");
const chatForm = document.getElementById("chat-form");
const userInput = document.getElementById("user-input");

// State
let isAwaitingResponse = false;

/**
 * Adds a message to the chat window.
 * @param {string} text - The message text.
 * @param {'user'|'bot'} sender - Who sent the message.
 */
function addMessage(text, sender) {
    const messageRow = document.createElement("div");
    messageRow.className = "message-row";

    const messageBubble = document.createElement("div");
    messageBubble.className = sender === "user" ? "message-user" : "message-bot";
    messageBubble.textContent = text;

    messageRow.appendChild(messageBubble);
    chatWindow.appendChild(messageRow);

    // Scroll to bottom
    chatWindow.scrollTop = chatWindow.scrollHeight;
}

/**
 * Shows a temporary loading indicator in the chat window.
 */
function showLoading() {
    const loadingRow = document.createElement("div");
    loadingRow.className = "message-row";
    loadingRow.id = "loading-indicator";

    const loadingBubble = document.createElement("div");
    loadingBubble.className = "message-bot";
    loadingBubble.innerHTML = `<span class="loading-dots">...</span>`;

    loadingRow.appendChild(loadingBubble);
    chatWindow.appendChild(loadingRow);

    chatWindow.scrollTop = chatWindow.scrollHeight;
}

/**
 * Removes the loading indicator from the chat window.
 */
function removeLoading() {
    const loadingIndicator = document.getElementById("loading-indicator");
    if (loadingIndicator) {
        chatWindow.removeChild(loadingIndicator);
    }
}

/**
 * Handles form submission: sends user message to backend and displays response.
 * @param {Event} e
 */
chatForm.addEventListener("submit", async function (e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    if (isAwaitingResponse) return;

    const message = userInput.value.trim();
    if (!message) return;

    addMessage(message, "user");
    userInput.value = "";
    userInput.disabled = true;
    isAwaitingResponse = true;
    showLoading();

    try {
        const response = await fetch(BACKEND_URL, {
            method: "POST",
            headers: {
                "Content-Type": "application/json"
            },
            body: JSON.stringify({ message })
        });

        removeLoading();
        userInput.disabled = false;
        isAwaitingResponse = false;

        if (!response.ok) {
            let errorMsg = "Network error. Please try again.";
            try {
                const errorData = await response.json();
                if (errorData.detail) errorMsg = errorData.detail;
            } catch (_) {}
            addMessage(errorMsg, "bot");
            return;
        }

        const data = await response.json();
        if (data && data.response) {
            addMessage(data.response, "bot");
        } else {
            addMessage("Sorry, I didn't understand that.", "bot");
        }
    } catch (err) {
        removeLoading();
        userInput.disabled = false;
        isAwaitingResponse = false;
        addMessage("Unable to connect to server. Please check your connection.", "bot");
    }
});

// Optional: Focus input on page load
window.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
    userInput.focus();
});

an existing plan to achieve the goal. A plan consists of one to 3 tasks.\nIf plan is created, you should track the progress and update the plan accordingly, such as Plan.finish_current_task, Plan.append_task, Plan.reset_task, Plan.replace_task, etc.\nWhen presented a current task, tackle the task using the available commands.\nPay close attention to new user message, review the conversation history, use RoleZero.reply_to_human to respond to new user requirement.\nNote:\n1. If you keeping encountering errors, unexpected situation, or you are not sure of proceeding, use RoleZero.ask_human to ask for help.\n2. "message-user" : "message-bot";\n    messageBubble.textContent = text;\n\n    messageRow.appendChild(messageBubble);\n    chatWindow.appendChild(messageRow);\n\n    // Scroll to bottom\n    chatWindow.scrollTop = chatWindow.scrollHeight;\n}\n\n/**\n * Shows a temporary loading indicator in the chat window.\n */\nfunction showLoading() {\n    const loadingRow = document.createElement("div");\n    loadingRow.className = "message-row";\n    loadingRow.id = "loading-indicator";\n\n    const loadingBubble = document.createElement("div");\n    loadingBubble.className = "message-bot";\n    loadingBubble.innerHTML = `<span class="loading-dots">...</span>`;\n\n    loadingRow.appendChild(loadingBubble);\n    chatWindow.appendChild(loadingRow);\n\n    chatWindow.scrollTop = chatWindow.scrollHeight;\n}\n\n/**\n * Removes the loading indicator from the chat window.\n */\nfunction removeLoading() {\n    const loadingIndicator = document.getElementById("loading-indicator");\n    if (loadingIndicator) {\n        chatWindow.removeChild(loadingIndicator);\n    }\n}\n\n/**\n * Handles form submission: sends user message to backend and displays response.\n * @param {Event} e\n */\nchatForm.addEventListener("submit", async function (e) {\n    e.preventDefault();\n    if (isAwaitingResponse) return;\n\n    const message = userInput.value.trim();\n    if (!message) return;\n\n    addMessage(message, "user");\n    userInput.value = "";\n    userInput.disabled = true;\n    isAwaitingResponse = true;\n    showLoading();\n\n    try {\n        const response = await fetch(BACKEND_URL, {\n            method: "POST",\n            headers: {\n                "Content-Type": "application/json"\n            },\n            body: JSON.stringify({ message })\n        });\n\n        removeLoading();\n        userInput.disabled = false;\n        isAwaitingResponse = false;\n\n        if (!response.ok) {\n            let errorMsg = "Network error. Please try again.";\n            try {\n                const errorData = await response.json();\n                if (errorData.detail) errorMsg = errorData.detail;\n            } catch (_) {}\n            addMessage(errorMsg, "bot");\n            return;\n        }\n\n        const data = await response.json();\n        if (data && data.response) {\n            addMessage(data.response, "bot");\n        } else {\n            addMessage("Sorry, I didn\'t understand that.", "bot");\n        }\n    } catch (err) {\n        removeLoading();\n        userInput.disabled = false;\n        isAwaitingResponse = false;\n        addMessage("Unable to connect to server. Please try again.";\n            try {\n                const errorData = await response.json();\n                if (errorData.detail) errorMsg = errorData.detail;\n            } catch (_) {}\n            addMessage(errorMsg, "bot");\n            return;\n        }\n\n        const data = await response.json();\n        if (data && data.response) {\n            addMessage(data.response, "bot");\n        } else {\n            addMessage("Sorry, I didn\'t understand that.", "bot");\n        }\n    } catch (err) {\n        removeLoading();\n        userInput.disabled = false;\n        isAwaitingResponse = false;\n        addMessage("Unable to connect to server. Please try again.";\n            try {\n                const errorData = await response.json();\n                if (errorData.detail) errorMsg = errorData.detail;\n            } catch (_) {}\n            addMessage(errorMsg, "bot");\n            return;\n        }\n\n        const data = await response.json();\n        if (data && data.response) {\n            addMessage(data.response, "bot");\n        } else {\n            addMessage("Sorry, I didn\'t understand that.", "bot");\n        }\n    } catch (err) {\n        removeLoading();\n        userInput.disabled = false;\n        isAwaitingResponse = false;\n        addMessage("Unable to connect to server. Please try again.";\n            try {\n                const errorData = await response.json();\n                if (errorData.detail) errorMsg = errorData.detail;\n            } catch (_) {}\n            addMessage(errorMsg, "bot");\n            return;\n        }\n\n        const data = await response.json();\n        if (data && data.response) {\n            addMessage(data.response, "bot");\n        } else {\n            addMessage("Sorry, I didn\'t understand that.", "bot");\n        }\n    } catch (err) {\n        removeLoading();\n        userInput.disabled = false;\n        isAwaitingResponse = false;\n        addMessage("Unable to connect to server.
